text: In her book Attention Span, Gloria Mark describes how the average length of time a person spends on a single screen before switching has collapsed from about two and a half minutes in 2004 to just 47 seconds by 2020. This isn't simply a story of worsening willpower — it reflects an arms race between human attentional limits and technology designed to exploit them. Platforms are optimized not for user wellbeing but for engagement metrics, deploying variable-ratio reinforcement schedules borrowed from behavioral psychology. The result is an environment that systematically degrades the capacity it depends on: a mind too fragmented to appreciate the very content designed to capture it.
question: How had the average time spent on a single screen before switching changed between 2004 and 2020, according to the passage?

options.text: It remained roughly stable at around two minutes throughout the period
It increased as users became more proficient at multitasking
It declined sharply, from about two and a half minutes to just 47 seconds
It halved once smartphones became the primary device for content consumption
